blocks.html:
PRESENTATION II
TOPOLOGIES AND BEAMSby J.L. Verdier

After the definition of topologies and pre-topologies (n° 1) and sheaves of sets (n° 2), we approach in n° 3 the central theorem of this presentation (3.4): the existence of the beam associated with a pre-beam. In order to cover all cases encountered in practice, the existence of this functor is demonstrated for presheaves on a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (3.0.2). Nos. 4 and 5 draw the consequences of this theorem on the behavior of inductive and projective limits in sheaf categories. At No. 6 we define and study the value sheaves in any categories, to immediately pay attention to the bundles of rings, groups, modules etc... .
1. Topologies, covering families, pretopologies

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

A topology on a category \(C\) is the data, for any object \(X\) of \(C\), of a set \(J(X)\) of screens of \(X\), this data being subject to the following axioms:

  1. (T 1)

    (Stability by change of base). For any object \(X\) of \(C\), any screen \(R \in J(X)\), any morphism \(f : Y \to X\) (\(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\,(C)\)), the sieve \(R \times_X Y\) of \(Y\) belongs to \(J(Y)\).

  2. (T 2)

    (Local character). If \(R\) and \(R'\) are two screens of \(X\), if \(R \in J(X)\), and if for all \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\,(C)\) and all morphism \(Y \to R\) the sieve \(R' \times_X Y\) belongs to \(J(Y)\), then \(R'\) belongs to \(J(X)\).

  3. (T 3)

    For any object \(X\) of \(C\), \(X\) belongs to \(J(X)\).

Screens belonging to \(J(X)\) will be called screens covering \(X\), or again the refinements of \(X\). From the axioms (T1), (T2), (T3), we immediately deduce that the set of sieves covering \(X\) is stable by finite intersections, and that every sieve containing a covering screen is a covering screen. The set \(J(X)\), ordered by inclusion, is therefore co-filtering (I 8).

Let \(C\) be a category, \(T\) and \(T'\) two topologies on \(C\). The topology \(T\) is said to be finer than the topology \(T'\) if for any object \(X\) of \(C\), any refinement of \(X\) for the topology \(T'\) is a refinement of \(X\) for topology \(T\). In this way, we define a order structure on all topologies.

Let \((T_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of topologies on \(C\). Sets, for any object \(X\) of \(C\), screens of \(X\) which are covering for all topologies \(T_i\), check the axioms (T 1), (T 2) and (T 3) and therefore define a topology: the intersection topology of \(T_i\), i.e. the lower bound of \(T_i\). It is the finest topology which is less fine that all topologies \(T_i\). The \((T_i)_{i \in I}\) family therefore admits aboundary superior: the topology intersection of topologies finer than each of \(T_i\).
In particular the data \(J(X) = \) the set of all the screens of \(X\), is a topology finer than any topology on \(C\), which we will call discrete topology.
There exists a topology less fine-grained than any topology on \(C\); the topology for which \(J(X) = \{X\}\) for all \(X\) of \(C\). This topology is called the coarse topology or chaotic.
A category \(C\) with a topology is called a site. Category \(C\) is called the category underlying the site.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2

Let \(C\) be a site, \(X\) be an object of \(C\). A family of morphisms \((f_\alpha : X_\alpha \to X)\), \(\alpha \in A\), is said to be covering if the sieve generated by the family \(f_\alpha\) (I 4.3.3) is a sieve covering \(X\).

Let \(C\) be a category. Let us give ourselves for each object \(X\) of \(C\), a set of families of goal morphisms \(X\). There then exists a topology \(T\) which is the least fine of the topologies for which the given families are covering, namely the intersection (1.1.3) of all the topologies in question. We call this topology thetopology generated by the sets of families of given morphisms. It is difficult to describe, in general, all covering screens of this topology. However, the situation is more nice in the following case:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

Let \(C\) be a category. A pretopology on \(C\) is the data, for each object \(X\) of \(C\), of a set \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) of families of goal morphisms \(X\), this data being subject to the following axioms:

  1. (PT 0)

    For any object \(X\) of \(C\), the morphisms of the families of morphisms of \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) are quatable. (Recall that a morphism \(Y \to X\) is said to be quarrable if for any morphism \(Z \to X\) the fiber product \(Z \times_X Y\) is representable).

  2. (PT 1)

    For any object \(X\) of \(C\), any family \((X_\alpha \to X)_{\alpha \in A}\) belonging to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\), and any morphism \(Y \to X\) (\(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\,(C)\)), the family: \((X_\alpha \times_X Y \to Y)_{\alpha \in A}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\). (Stability by change of base).

  3. (PT 2)

    If \((X_\alpha \to X)_{\alpha \in A}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) and if for each \(\alpha \in A\), \((X_{\beta_\alpha} \to X_\alpha)_{\beta_\alpha \in B_\alpha}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X_\alpha)\), then the family \((X_\gamma \to X)_{\gamma \in \coprod_{\alpha \in A} B_\alpha}\) (where for all \(\gamma = (\alpha, \beta_\alpha)\), \(\alpha \in A\), \(\beta_\alpha \in B_\alpha\), the morphism \(X_\gamma \to X\) is the compound morphism: \(X_\gamma = X_{\beta_\alpha} \to X_\alpha \to X\)) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\). (Stability by composition).

  4. (PT 3)

    Family: \(X \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id}_X\ } X\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\).

The definition of 1.1.6 allows us to consider, for a given pretopology on \(C\), the topology on \(C\) generated by this pretopology. Note that if \(C\) is a category where the fiber products are representable, then any topology \(T\) of \(C\) can be defined by a pretopology, namely the one for which \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) is formed of all families covering \(X\) for topology \(T\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.4

Let \(C\) be a category, \(E\) be a pretopology on \(C\), \(T\) the topology defined by the pretopology \(E\) (1.1.6), \(X\) an object of \(C\). Let us denote by \(J_E(X)\) the set of screens of \(X\) generated by the families of the pretopology, and by \(J_T(X)\) all refinements of \(X\) for the topology \(T\). Then \(J_E(X)\) is cofinal in \(J_T(X)\). In other words, for a sieve \(R\) of \(X\) to belong to \(J_T(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient that it exists a screen \(R' \in J_E(X)\) such that \(R' \subset R\).

Proof. Let, for any object \(X\) of \(C\), \(J'(X)\) be the set of screens of \(X\) which contain a screen of \(J_E(X)\). We obviously have \(J'(X) \subset J_T(X)\). To show that \(J'(X) = J_T(X)\), it suffices to show that the data of \(J'(X)\) defines a topology on \(C\). Gold the \(J'(X)\) obviously verify the axioms (T 1) and (T 3). It therefore remains to be verified (T 2). For this suffices to demonstrate that if \(R'\) is a sub-screen of \(R \in J_E(X)\) such that for all \(Y \to R\), the screen \(R' \times_X Y\) of \(Y\) belongs to \(J'(X)\), the screen \(R'\) belongs to \(J'(X)\). But the sieve \(R\) is spawned by a family \((X_\alpha \to X)\) belonging to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\), and for all \(\alpha\) the sieve \(R' \times_X X_\alpha\) contains a sieve generated by a family \((X_{\beta_\alpha} \to X_\alpha)\) belonging to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X_\alpha)\). We deduce that the sieve \(R'\) contains a sieve generated by the family \((X_{\beta_\alpha} \to X)\). Therefore, according to the axiom (PT 2), \(R'\) contains a sieve of \(J_E(X)\) and consequently belongs to \(J'(X)\), cqfd.
2. Set bundles

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1

Let \(C\) be a site whose underlying category is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. A pre-bundle \(F\) with values in \(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) is said to be separated (resp. is a beam) if for any screen \(R\) covering \(X\), object of \(C\), the application: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F) \] is an injection (resp. a bijection). The full subcategory of \(\widehat{C}\) whose objects are the sheaves is called the category of set sheaves on \(C\), and is denoted the more often \(\widetilde{C}\) (1). When no ambiguity results, we will say simply beam category on \(C\); on the other hand we will sometimes specify in saying category of sheaves with value in \(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) or even category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(\mathscr{F} = (F_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-bundles on \(C\). Let us designate, for each object \(X\) of \(C\), by \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\) all of the sifters \(R \hookrightarrow X\) of \(C\) such that for any morphism \(Y \to X\) of \(C\) of goal \(X\), the sieve \(R \times_X Y\) has the following property: the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,F_i) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R \times_X Y, F_i) \] is bijective (resp. injective) for all \(i \in I\). Then the sets \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\) define a topology on \(C\), which is the finest topology for which each of \(F_i\) is a beam (resp. a separate pre-beam).
Proof. The \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\) obviously verify axioms (T 1) and (T 3). It remains to show that they verify (T 2). To do this, it is enough to show that:

1) If \(R' \hookrightarrow R \hookrightarrow X\) are two sifters of \(X\), such that \(R \in J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\) and such that for any \(Y \to R\) (where \(Y\) is an object of \(C\)) the sieve \(R' \times_X Y\) belongs to \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(Y)\), then the screen \(R'\) belongs to \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\).
2) If \(R' \hookrightarrow R \hookrightarrow X\) are two sifters of \(X\) such that \(R' \in J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\), then the sifter \(R\) belongs to \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\).

Note that, in case 2), for all \(Y \to R\) (where \(Y\) is an object of \(C\)) the sieve \(R' \times_X Y\) belongs to \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(Y)\). Now, in cases 1) and 2), \(R\) is the inductive limit of \(Y\), objects of \(C\), above \(R\) (I 3.4). The inductive limits in \(\widehat{C}\) are universal (I 3.3). By following in cases 1) and 2), \(R'\) is the inductive limit of \(R' \times_X Y\). However, in cases 1) and (2), \(R' \times_X Y\) belongs to \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(Y)\). We deduce, by passing to the inductive limit on the objects \(Y\) of \(C\) above \(R\), that the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F_i) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R',F_i) \] is a bijection (resp. an injection) for all \(i \in I\). Consequently, the applications \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,F_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R',F_i) \quad\text{et}\quad \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,F_i) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F_i) \] are, in cases 1) and 2), bijections (resp. injections). Additionally, assumptions 1) and 2) are visibly stable by any base change \(Y \to X\) (\(Y\) object of \(C\)). We then deduces that for any object \(Y\) of \(C\) above \(X\) and all \(i \in I\), the applications \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,F_i) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R \times_X Y, F_i) \] \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,F_i) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R' \times_X Y, F_i) \] are in both cases bijections (resp. injections); which shows that \(R'\) and \(R\) belong to \(J_{\mathscr{F}}(X)\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and for all \(X\) of \(C\) a set \(K(X)\) of \(X\) screens. We assume that the \(K(X)\) satisfy the axiom (T 1) of (1.1). So that a pre-bundle \(F\) is a beam (resp. a separate pre-bundle) for the generated topology (1.1.6) by \(K(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient that for any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any sieve \(R \in K(X)\), the app \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F) \] or a bijection (resp. an injection).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

In particular, let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category with a pretopology. For a pre-beam \(F\) to be a beam (resp. a separate pre-beam), it It is necessary and sufficient that for any object \(X\) of \(C\) and for any family \((X_\alpha \to X)\) belonging to \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\), the set diagram \[ F(X) \longrightarrow \prod_{\alpha \in A} F(X_\alpha) \rightrightarrows \prod_{(\alpha,\beta) \in A \times A} F(X_\alpha \times_X X_\beta) \] is exact (resp. the application \[ F(X) \longrightarrow \prod_{\alpha \in A} F(X_\alpha) \] or injective).
Proof. We apply 2.3, then (I 3.5) and (I 2.12).
We find with corollary 2.4 the definition given in [1].

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.5

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. We call canonical topology of \(C\) the finest topology for which the representable functors are sheaves (2.2). A screen covering \(X\) for the canonical topology will be calledepimorphic screen strict universal. A covering family for the canonical topology will be calledfamily universal strict epimorphic. When in addition the morphisms of the covering family are quatable, the family will be called universal effective epimorphic [2].

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 2.5.1

For almost all the sites we have had to use until present, the topology is less fine-grained than the canonical topology, in other words, the functors representable on \(C\) are sheaves, i.e. the covering families of \(C\) are universal strict epimorphics. The only exception to this rule is the site \(\widehat{C}\) studied at No. 5 (whose topology is finer, and most often strictly finer, than the canonical topology).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.6

So that a \(R \hookrightarrow X\) sieve is strict epimorphic universal, it is necessary and sufficient that for any object \(Y \to X\) of \(C\) above \(X\), and for all object \(Z\) of \(C\), the application: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_C(Y,Z) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_{\widehat{C}/(Y \times_X R)} \mathrm{Hom}_C(\,\cdot\,, Z) \] or a bijection.
Proof. Immediately by applying 2.2 then (I 5.3).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.7

1) Proposition 2.6 gives a characterization of epimorphic sieves strict universals of a category \(C\), independent of the universe in which the pre-sheaves take their values, on the sole condition that the sets of morphisms \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\) of \(C\) belong to this universe. It thus makes it possible to define the canonical topology for any category \(C\), without it being necessary to specify the universes.

2) Let \(C\) be a site whose underlying category is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(F\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundle and \(\mathcal{V}\) a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\). The category underlying \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-category, and \(F\) can be considered a \(\mathcal{V}\)-prebundle. The \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-beam \(F\) is a beam (resp. a separate pre-beam) if and only if the \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-beam \(F\) is a beam (resp. a separate pre-bundle). In other words, the condition for a pre-bundle \(F\) to be a beam (resp. a separate pre-beam) does not depend (in the sense just specified) on the universe in which the pre-bundle \(F\) takes its values. In particular are \(C\) a site and \(F\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundle; we say that \(F\) is a beam (resp. a separate pre-beam) if there exists a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) containing \(\mathcal{U}\) such that the category \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-category and such that \(F\) is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundle (resp. a separate \(\mathcal{V}\)-prebundle). This property does not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{V}\).

footnotes.html: (1) or \(C^{\sim}\), depending on the mood of the typewriter.

blocks.html: 3. Harness associated with a pre-harness

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.0.1

Let \(C\) be a site. We callfamily topologically generator (or when no confusion results, generating family) of \(C\), a set \(G\) of objects of \(C\) such that any object of \(C\) is the goal of a covering family of morphisms of \(C\) (1.2) whose sources are elements of \(G\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.0.2

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. We call \(\mathcal{U}\)-site a site \(C\) whose the underlying category is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category (I 1.1), which has a small family topologically generative. Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category; we call \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology on \(C\) a topology on \(C\) making \(C\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. We say that a site \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, or by abuse of language, small, if the category underlying \(C\) is small (I 1.0).
It immediately follows from the definitions that any topology finer than a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology, and a small site is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.0.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(G\) a small topologically generating family from \(C\). For all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), let us designate by \(J_G(X)\) the set of screens covering \(X\) generated by a family of morphisms \((Y_\alpha \xrightarrow{\ u_\alpha\ } X)\), \(\alpha \in A\), where \(Y_\alpha \in G\).

1) The set \(J_G(X)\) is small.

2) The set \(J_G(X)\) is cofinal in the set \(J(X)\) of all screens covering \(X\), ordered by inclusion.

3) For all \(R \in J_G(X)\), there exists a small epimorphic family (I 10.2) \((u_\alpha : Y \to R)\), \(\alpha \in A\), with \(Y \in G\).
Proof. 1) Let's set \(A(X) = \coprod_{Y \in G} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\). The set \(A(X)\) is small (I 0) and \(\mathrm{card}(J_G(X)) \leq 2^{\mathrm{card}(A(X))}\). As a result \(J_G(X)\) is small.

2) Let \(R \in J(X)\). Let us set \(A(R) = \coprod_{Y \in G} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,R)\) and let \(R'\) be the sieve of \(X\) sired by family \((Y \xrightarrow{\ u\ } R \hookrightarrow X)\), \(u \in A(R)\). We have \(R' \subset R\) and we just need to show that \(R'\) is covering. According to axiom (T2) of 1.1, it suffices to show that for any morphism \(Z \to R\), \(Z \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the sieve \(R' \times_X Z\) of \(Z\) is covering. Gold the sieve \(R' \times_X Z\) contains the sieve generated by the family of all morphisms \(Y \to Z\) with \(Y \in G\), family which is, by hypothesis, covering. The screen \(R' \times_X Z\) of \(Z\) is therefore covering (axiom T2 of 1.1).

3) Let \(R \in J_G(X)\). The family \((Y \xrightarrow{\ u\ } R)\), \(u \in A(R) = \coprod_{Y \in G} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,R)\) is, by hypothesis, epimorphic. Now, for all \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,R)\) is contained in \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\) which is small. As a result \(A(R)\) is small.

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(C \in \mathcal{V}\) and \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\), \(G\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small topologically generating family of \(C\). The \(\widehat{C}\) category of pre-bundles \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets on \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-category (I 1.1.1). Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\). All \(J(X)\) of sieves covering \(X\) is \(\mathcal{V}\)-small and, ordered by inclusion, it is cofiltering (1.1.1). For everything \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-beam \(F\), the inductive limit \(\varinjlim_{J(X)} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F)\) is therefore representable by a element of \(\mathcal{V}\) (I 2.4.1). Furthermore, it follows from (3.0.4 3)) that, for all \(R \in J_G(X)\), \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F)\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, and since \(J_G(X)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small cofinal set in \(J(X)\) (loc. cit.), it follows from (I 2.4.2) that \(\varinjlim \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F)\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. Let us then choose, for all \(F\) and for all \(X\), an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) which represents this inductive limit and let us put \[ LF(X) = \varinjlim_{J(X)} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(\,\cdot\,, F) . \] Let \(g : Y \to X\) be a morphism of \(C\). The change of base functor \(g^* : J(X) \to J(Y)\) defines an application: \[ LF(g) : LF(X) \longrightarrow LF(Y) \] which makes \(X \mapsto LF(X)\) a pre-beam on \(C\).

The morphism \(\mathrm{id}_X : X \to X\) being an element of \(J(X)\) we have, for any object \(X\) of \(C\), an application: \[ \ell(F)(X) : F(X) \longrightarrow LF(X), \] visibly defining a morphism of functors: \[ \ell(F) : F \longrightarrow LF . \] It is further clear that \(F \mapsto LF\) is a functor in \(F\) and that the morphisms \(\ell(F)\) define a morphism \[ \ell : \mathrm{Id} \longrightarrow L . \] Finally, let \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be a refinement of \(X\). The definition of \(LF(X)\) and (I 1.4) gives us provide application: \[ Z_R : \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,LF) , \] and for any morphism of \(C\), \(Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } X\), the definition of the functor \(LF\) shows us that the diagram below, is commutative: \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ Z_R : \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,LF) \ar[d] \\ Z_{R \times_X Y} : \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R \times_X Y, F) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,LF) } \] (the vertical arrows are the obvious arrows).
Let's then copy [2].

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

1) For any refinement \(i_R : R \hookrightarrow X\) and all \(u : R \to F\), the diagram: \[ (**) \qquad \xymatrix{ F \ar[r]^-{\ell(F)} & LF \\ R \ar[u]^-{u} \ar[r]_-{i_R} & X \ar[u]_-{Z_R(u)} } \] is commutative.

2) For any morphism \(v : X \to LF\), there exists a refinement \(R\) of \(X\) and a morphism \(u : R \to F\) such that \(Z_R(u) = v\).

3) Let \(Y\) be an object of \(C\) and \(u, v : Y \rightrightarrows F\) two morphisms such that \(\ell(F) \circ u = \ell(F) \circ v\). Then the core of the couple \((u,v)\) is a refinement of \(Y\).

4) Let \(R\) and \(R'\) be two refinements of \(X\), \(u : R \to F\) and \(u' : R' \to F\) two morphisms. For \(Z_R(u) = Z_{R'}(u')\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(u\) and \(u'\) coincide on a refinement \(R'' \to R \times_X R'\).

Proof. The only non-trivial assertion is assertion 1). It must be shown that \(Z_R(u) \circ i_R = \ell(F) \circ u\). For this it suffices to show (I 3.4) that the compounds of these morphisms with any morphism \(Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } R\) (\(Y\) object of \(C\)) are equal. Now, consider \(f = i_R \circ g\) and the fiber product \(R \times_X Y\): \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[r]^-{\ell(F)} & LF \\ R \ar[u]^-{u} \ar[r]^-{T_R} & X \ar[u]_-{Z_R(u)} \\ R \times_X Y \ar[u]^-{g'} \ar[r]_-{i'}^-{\sim} & Y \ar[u]_-{f} } \] In this diagram above, \(i'\) is a monomorphism which admits one section. As a result (1.3.3) \(i'\) is an isomorphism. Now by definition of the morphism \(\ell(F)\), the morphism \(Z_{R \times_X Y}(u \circ g')\) is equal to \(\ell(F) \circ u \circ g\). On the other hand the commutativity of the diagram (*) provides us with the equality \(Z_{R \times_X Y}(u \circ g') = Z_R(u) \circ f\), and as a result we have the equality \(\ell(F) \circ u \circ g = Z_R(u) \circ i_R \circ g\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2

1) The functor \(L\) is left exact (I 2.3.2).

2) For any pre-bundle \(F\), \(LF\) is a separate pre-bundle.

3) The presheaf \(F\) is separated if and only if the morphism \(\ell(F) : F \to LF\) is a monomorphism. The pre-beam \(LF\) is then a beam.

4) The following properties are equivalent:

i) \(\ell(F) : F \to LF\) is an isomorphism.

ii) \(F\) is a beam.
Proof

1) It suffices to show (I 3.1) that for any object \(X\) of \(C\), the functor \(F \mapsto LF(X)\) commutes at finite projective limits. Now by definition of the projective limit, the functor \[ F \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F) \qquad R \hookrightarrow X \in \mathrm{ob}(J(X)) \] commutes at projective limits, and the inductive limit \(\varinjlim_{J(X)}\) commutes at limits finite projectives because \(J(X)\) is a cofiltering set (I 2.7).

2) Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) and \(f, g : X \rightrightarrows LF\) two morphisms which coincide on a refinement \(R \hookrightarrow X\) of \(X\). By virtue of 3.1(2), there exists a sieve covering \(R' \hookrightarrow X\), which we can always assume contained in \(R\), and two morphisms \(u, v : R' \rightrightarrows F\) such that \(Z_{R'}(u) = f\) and \(Z_{R'}(v) = g\). By virtue of 3.1 1) we then have \(\ell(F) \circ u = \ell(F) \circ v\). As a result (3.1 4)) \(u\) and \(v\) coincide on a refinement \(R'' \hookrightarrow R'\). Let \(w\) be the restriction of \(u\) to \(R''\). We have \(Z_{R''}(w) = Z_{R'}(u) = Z_{R'}(v)\) and subsequently \(f = g\). The pre-bundle \(LF\) is therefore separated.

3) If \(F\) is separated, the morphism \(\ell(F)\) is a monomorphism because a limit inductive filtering of monomorphisms is a monomorphism. If \(\ell(F)\) is a monomorphism, the prebundle \(F\) is a sub-prebundle of a separate prebundle, so it is separated. Let us show that \(LF\) is then a sheaf. Let \(i : R \hookrightarrow X\) be a sieve covering a object \(X\) of \(C\), and \(u : R \to LF\) a morphism. We just need to show that \(u\) is factorized by \(X\). Let's set \(R' = F \times_{LF} R\) and \(v = Z_{R'}(\mathrm{pr}_1)\): \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[r]^-{\ell(F)} & LF & \\ R' \ar[u]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & R \ar[u]^-{u} \ar[r]^-{i} & X \ar[ul]_-{v} \\ R'' \ar[u]^-{p_1} \ar[r]^-{p_2} & Y \ar[u]^-{m} & } \] To show that \(u = v \circ i\), it suffices to show (I 3.4) that for any morphism \(m : Y \to R\) (\(Y\) object of \(C\)), \(v \circ i \circ m = u \circ m\). Let us set \(R'' = Y \times_R R'\) and let \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) be the projections. The projection \(R'' \xrightarrow{\ p_2\ } Y\) is a monomorphism and makes \(R''\) a sieve covering \(Y\) (3.1.2)). We have \(v \circ i \circ \mathrm{pr}_2 = \ell(F) \circ \mathrm{pr}_1\) (3.1 1)), and consequently \(v \circ i \circ \mathrm{pr}_2 = u \circ \mathrm{pr}_2\). We deduce \(v \circ i \circ \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ p_1 = u \circ \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ p_1\) i.e. \(v \circ i \circ m \circ p_2 = u \circ m \circ p_2\). As the pre-bundle \(LF\) is separated, we have \(v \circ i \circ m = u \circ m\), cqfd.
4) Clear.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.3

Let \(J'(X)\) be a cofinal subset of \(J(X)\). We have \[ LF(X) = \varinjlim_{J'(X)} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(\,\cdot\,, F) . \] In particular, if the topology of \(C\) is defined by a pretopology \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Cov}(X)\) (1.1.3), the functor \(L\) can be described using the covering families of \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) (I 2.12 and I 3.5). In explaining the formulas we find the construction of [1].

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. The inclusion functor \(i : \widetilde{C} \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}\) of the bundles in the pre-sheaves admits an adjoint on the left \(a\), exact on the left (I 2.3.2): \[ \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C} \ar@{^{(}->}@<-0.7ex>[r]_-{i} & \widehat{C} \ar@<-0.7ex>[l]_-{a} } \] The functor \(i \circ a\) is canonically isomorphic to the functor \(L \circ L\) (cf. 3.0.5). For everything presheaf \(F\) the addition morphism \(F \to i \circ a(F)\) is deduced by the isomorphism precedent of the morphism \(\ell(LF) \circ \ell(F) : F \longrightarrow L \circ L(F)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.5

The beam \(aF\) is called thebeam associated with the prebeam \(F\).
Theorem 3.4 follows immediately from proposition 3.2.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.6

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(\mathcal{V} \supset \mathcal{U}\) be a universe. Let us note \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) and \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) (resp. \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) and \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\)) the categories of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-bundles and \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundles (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-bundles and \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundles) and \(a_{\mathcal{U}} : \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) (resp. \(a_{\mathcal{V}} : \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\)) the corresponding “associated bundles” functors. The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[r]^-{a_{\mathcal{U}}} \ar@{^{(}->}[d] & \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar@{^{(}->}[d] \\ \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \ar[r]^-{a_{\mathcal{V}}} & \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}} } \] where the vertical functors are the canonical inclusions, is commutative to isomorphism near canonical.
Proof. Result of the construction of the functors \(a_{\mathcal{U}}\) and \(a_{\mathcal{V}}\) (3.4 and 3.0.5).
4. Beam Category Accuracy Properties

The accuracy properties of the category of sheaves are deduced from the properties of accuracy of the category of pre-sheaves via Theorem 3.4. This issue explains this philosophy in some of the most useful standard statements.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(\widetilde{C}\) the category of beams, \(a : \widehat{C} \to \widetilde{C}\) the associated beam functor, \(i : \widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C}\) the inclusion functor.

1) The functor \(a\) commutes at inductive limits and is exact.

2) The \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits in \(\widetilde{C}\) are representable. For little ones category \(I\) and for any functor \(E : I \to \widetilde{C}\), the canonical morphism \[ \varinjlim_I E \longrightarrow a(\varinjlim_I i \circ E) \] is an isomorphism.

3) The \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective limits in \(\widetilde{C}\) are representable. For any object \(X\) of \(C\), the functor on \(\widetilde{C}\): \(F \mapsto F(X)\) commutes to projective limits, i.e. the functor of inclusion \(i : \widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C}\) commutes to projective limits.
Proof. These properties result essentially from Theorem 3.4 and (I 2.11).

Thus, in the bundle category, products indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), fiber products, sums indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), amalgamated sums, kernels, cokernels, images, coimages are representable.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.1.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(C\). Canonical homomorphism \[ \varinjlim_{C/F} aX \longrightarrow F \] is an isomorphism.
Proof. Result from (I 3.4) and the fact that \(a\) commutes at inductive limits.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.2

Any morphism of \(\widetilde{C}\), which is both an epimorphism and a monomorphism, is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let \(u : G \to H\) be a morphism of \(\widetilde{C}\) which is an epimorphism and a monomorphism. Let us first notice that the morphism \(u\) is a monomorphism of pre-bundles (4.1 1)). Let us construct the amalgamated sum \(H \coprod_G H = K\) and both canonical morphisms \(i_1, i_2 : H \rightrightarrows K\) in the category of presheaves. As \(u\) is a monomorphism of pre-sheaves, we immediately verify that the diagram below is Cartesian and co-Cartesian (I 10.1): \[ \xymatrix{ G \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{u} & H \ar[d]^-{i_2} \\ H \ar[r]_-{i_1} & K } \] By applying the “associated beam” functor, we therefore obtain a Cartesian diagram and cocartesian of the category of sheaves (4.1 1)): \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ G \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{u} & H \ar[d]^-{a i_2} \\ H \ar[r]_-{a i_1} & aK } \] As \(u\) is an epimorphism of sheaves, the morphism \(a i_1\) is an isomorphism and as the diagram (*) is Cartesian, the morphism \(u\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.3

1) The inductive limits in \(\widetilde{C}\) which are representable, are universal (I 2.5).

2) Any epimorphic family (I 10.2) of morphisms is effective epimorphic universal (2.6).

3) Any equivalence relation is effectively universal (I 10.6).

4) The \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering inductive limits commute to finite projective limits (I 2.6).

Proof. Assertions 1) to 4) are true in the category of sets therefore in the category of pre-bundles (I 3.1). Assertions 1) and 4) then immediately result corresponding assertions for the pre-beams and 4.1. Let us demonstrate 3). Either \(p_1, p_2 : R \rightrightarrows X\) an equivalence relation of \(\widetilde{C}\). The diagram \(R \rightrightarrows X\) is then a pre-bundle equivalence relation (4.1 1)). There therefore exists a morphism of presheaves \(u : X \to Y\) such as the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{p_2} \ar[d]_-{p_1} & X \ar[d]^-{u} \\ X \ar[r]_-{u} & Y } \] either Cartesian and co-Cartesian in the category of pre-sheaves. By applying the functor “associated beam”, we obtain a Cartesian and co-Cartesian diagram in the category of beams (4.1 1)). Consequently \(R \rightrightarrows X\) is an effective equivalence relation. Like all equivalence relation is effective according to the above, every equivalence relation is universal effective. Let us demonstrate 2). Let \((u_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), be an epimorphic family of \(\widetilde{C}\). According to (II 2.6) and (I 2.12), it is enough to prove the following assertions:
a) For any sheaf morphism \(v : Y \to X\), the family of morphisms \(\mathrm{pr}_{2,i} : X_i \times_X Y \to Y\), \(i \in I\), is an epimorphic family of \(\widetilde{C}\).

b) For any sheaf \(Z\) the set diagram: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,Z) \longrightarrow \prod_{i \in I} \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,Z) \rightrightarrows \coprod_{(i,k) \in I \times I} \mathrm{Hom}(X_i \times_X X_k, Z) \] is correct.

Let \(X'\) be the union of the images of the morphisms \(u_i\) in the sense of the presheaves and \(j : X' \hookrightarrow X\) canonical injection. For all \(i \in I\), the morphism \(u_i : X_i \to X\) is factorized into a morphism \(u'_i : X_i \to X'\) and the morphism \(j\), and the family \(u'_i : X_i \to X'\), \(i \in I\), is a epimorphic family of \(\widehat{C}\). Since \(j\) is a monomorphism, the morphism \(aj : aX' \to X\) is a monomorphism of sheaves (4.1). As for all \(i \in I\), we have \(u_i = aj \circ au'_i\), \(aj\) is a epimorphism of bundles. Consequently \(aj\) is an isomorphism (4.2). Let \(v : Y \to X\) be a morphism of sheaves. We deduce by change of base a morphism \(j_v : X' \times_X Y \to Y\) and morphisms \(u'_{i,v} : X_i \times_X Y \to X' \times_X Y\). As \(a\) switches to fiber products, \(aj_v\) is an isomorphism. Like the epimorphic families of \(\widehat{C}\) preserve this character by base change, the family \(u'_{i,v}\), \(i \in I\), is epimorphic in \(\widehat{C}\). As \(a\) commutes at inductive limits, the family \(au'_{i,v} : X_i \times_X Y \longrightarrow a(X' \times_X Y)\), \(i \in I\), is epimorphic in \(\widetilde{C}\) hence a). Let us demonstrate b). Let \(Z\) be a beam. As \(aj\) is an isomorphism, the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,Z) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(X',Z) \] is a bijection. As the \(u'_i\), \(i \in I\), form an epimorphic family of \(\widehat{C}\), the overview diagram: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X',Z) \longrightarrow \prod_{i \in I} \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,Z) \rightrightarrows \coprod_{(i,k) \in I \times I} \mathrm{Hom}(X_i \times_X X_k, Z) \] is correct. Finally as \(X'\) is a sub-prebundle of \(X\), the prebundle \(X_i \times_{X'} X_k\), \((i,k) \in I \times I\), is canonically isomorphic to \(X_i \times_X X_k\), hence b).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 4.3.1

1) Proposition 4.3 2) formally provides us with a second demonstration of 4.2: It is clear that, in any category, a morphism which is both a universal effective epimorphism and a monomorphism is actually an isomorphism.

2) We deduce from the previous propositions that any morphism in the category of bundles uniquely factorize into an effective epimorphism and a monomorphism effective. This property generalizes in a natural way, for non-additive categories, the axiom (AB2) of abelian categories (\(\mathrm{coim} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{im}\)) [Tohoku].

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. The functor \(h : C \to \widehat{C}\) (I 1.3.1), composed with the functor associated bundle, provides a functor \[ \varepsilon_C : C \longrightarrow \widetilde{C} , \] called canonical functor of \(C\) in \(\widetilde{C}\), which will be constantly used thereafter. The functor \(\varepsilon_C\) commutes at finite projective limits. When the topology of \(C\) is less fine that the canonical topology, \(\varepsilon_C\) is fully faithful, and commutes at projective limits; he is then, moreover, defined when \(C\) does not necessarily have a small family topologically generative. We will not study in detail the behavior of \(\varepsilon_C\) by relation to inductive limits (cf. [SGA 3 IV]). However, we will need suggestions below.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \((u_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a family of morphisms from \(C\) to goal \(X\). The following conditions are equivalent (∗):

i) The family \((\varepsilon_C u_i : \varepsilon_C X_i \longrightarrow \varepsilon_C X)\), \(i \in I\), is an epimorphic family of \(\widetilde{C}\) (I 10.2).

ii) The family \((u_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), is a covering family of \(C\) (1.2).

Proof. ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). Let \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be the sieve generated by \(u_i\), \(i \in I\) (I 4.3.3), and \(u'_i : X_i \to R\) the morphisms induced by \(u_i\). The family of \(u'_i\), \(i \in I\), is a family epimorphic of \(\widehat{C}\), and \(R\) is a sieve covering \(X\). Consequently, for any beam \(F\), the app \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R,F) \] is bijective and the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(R,F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,F) \] is injective. So the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,F) \] is injective and therefore the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(aX,F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(aX_i,F) \] is injective, hence i).

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). With the notations introduced previously, let \(J_R : R \hookrightarrow X\) be the injection canonical in \(X\) of the sieve generated by \(u_i\), \(i \in I\). It follows from i) that the morphism of fascicles \(aJ_R : aR \to aX\) is both an epimorphism of fascicles and a monomorphism of beams. It is therefore an isomorphism of sheaves (4.2). We have, with the notations of No. 3, a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{\ell(X)} & LX \ar[r]^-{\ell(LX)} & aX \\ R \ar[u]^-{J_R} \ar[r]_-{\ell(R)} & LR \ar[u]^-{LJ_R} \ar[r]_-{\ell(LR)} & aR \ar[u]_-{aJ_R}^-{\wr} } \] First of all, according to 3.1 2), there exists a sieve covering \(J_{S_1} : S_1 \hookrightarrow X\) and a morphism \(u_1 : S_1 \to LR\) such that \[ (4.6.1) \qquad \ell(LX) \circ \ell(X) \circ J_{S_1} = aJ_R \circ \ell(LR) \circ u_1 = \ell(LX) \circ LJ_R \circ u_1 . \] Let's set \(m = \ell(X) \circ J_{S_1}\) and \(n = LJ_R \circ u_1\). The morphisms \(m\) and \(n\) are morphisms of \(S_1\) in \(LX\). Let \(S_2 \hookrightarrow S_1\) be the kernel of the pair of arrows \((m,n)\). For any object \(Y\) of \(C\) and all morphism \(\alpha : Y \to S_1\), we have, by virtue of (4.6.1), \(\ell(LX) \circ m \circ \alpha = \ell(LX) \circ n \circ \alpha\). It results then from (3.1.3)) that the kernel \(S_2 \times_{S_1} Y\) of the pair of arrows \((m \circ \alpha, n \circ \alpha)\) is a sieve covering \(Y\). Consequently, according to the axiom (T2) of topologies, \(J_{S_2} : S_2 \hookrightarrow X\) is a sieve covering \(X\). We therefore have a morphism \(u_2 : S_2 \to LR\) and a commutative diagram: \[ (4.6.2) \qquad \xymatrix{ S_2 \ar[rr]^-{J_{S_2}} \ar[dd]_-{u_2} & & X \ar[dd]^-{\ell(X)} \\ & R \ar[ur]_-{J_R} \ar[dl]^-{\ell(R)} & \\ LR \ar[rr]_-{LJ_R} & & LX } \] Let \(Y\) be an object of \(C\) and \(\beta : Y \to S_2\) be a morphism. According to 3.1 1), there exists a sieve covering \(J_{Q_1} : Q_1 \hookrightarrow Y\) and a morphism \(v_1 : Q_1 \to LR\) such that \(u_2 \circ \beta \circ J_{Q_1} = \ell(R) \circ v_1\). Let's set \(f = J_{S_2} \circ \beta \circ J_{Q_1}\), \(g = J_R \circ v_1\). Let \(Q_2\) be the core of couple \((f,g) : Q_1 \rightrightarrows X\) and \(J_{Q_2} : Q_2 \hookrightarrow Y\), canonical injection. For any object \(Z\) of \(C\) and all morphism \(\gamma : Z \to Q_1\), we have, by virtue of the commutativity of (4.6.2): \[ \ell(X) \circ f \circ \gamma = \ell(X) \circ J_{S_2} \circ \beta \circ J_{Q_1} \circ \gamma = LJ_R \circ u_2 \circ \beta \circ J_{Q_1} \circ \gamma = \] \[ = LJ_R \circ \ell(R) \circ v_1 \circ \gamma = \ell(X) \circ J_R \circ v_1 \circ \gamma = \ell(X) \circ g \circ \gamma . \] It then follows from 3.1.2) that the kernel \(Q_2 \times_{Q_1} Z\) of the couple \((f \circ \gamma, g \circ \gamma)\) is a sieve covering \(Z\). According to the axiom (T 2) of topologies, \(J_{Q_2} : Q_2 \hookrightarrow Y\) is a sieve covering \(Y\). For any morphism \(\beta : Y \to S\), there therefore exists a sieve covering \(J_{Q_2} : Q_2 \hookrightarrow Y\) and a morphism \(v_2 : Q_2 \to R\) such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[r]^-{\beta} & S_2 \ar[r]^-{J_{S_2}} & X \\ Q_2 \ar[u]^-{J_{Q_2}} \ar[rr]_-{v_2} & & R \ar[u]_-{J_R} } \] Let us then denote \(S_2 \cap R\) the fiber product of \(S_2\) and \(R\) above \(X\). The sieve (from \(Y\)): \((S_2 \cap R) \times_{S_2} Y\) contains the sieve \(Q_2\) and therefore \((S_2 \cap R) \times_{S_2} Y\) is a sieve covering \(Y\). He then results from the axiom (T 2) of topologies that \(S_2 \cap R\) is a sieve covering \(X\) and consequently the sieve \(R\) which contains \(S_2 \cap R\) is a sieve covering \(X\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4.4

Let \(T\) be the topology of \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\), \(T'\) (resp. \(T''\)) the finest topology on \(C\) among those for which the objects of \(\widetilde{C}\) are sheaves (resp. pre-beams) (2.2). So \(T = T' = T''\).
Indeed, we trivially have \(T \leq T' \leq T''\), and 4.4 and 2.2 imply that \(T'' \leq T\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.5

An initial object of a category \(A\) is an object \(\varnothing_A\) which represents the empty inductive limit i.e. such that for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, A\), there exists an arrow and an only \(\varnothing_A \to X\). We say that an object \(\varnothing_A\) is strict initial if it is initial and if all goal morphism \(\varnothing_A\) is an isomorphism. Let \((S_i)\), \(i \in I\), be a family of objects of a category \(A\). Suppose the sum \(s = \coprod_{i \in I} S_i\) is representable. It is said that the sum \(S\) is disjoint if the structural morphisms \(S_i \to S\) are quatable, if they are monomorphisms and if for any pair \(i,j\), \(i \neq j\) the product \(S_i \times_S S_j\) is an initial object from \(A\). We say that the sum \(S\) is universal disjoint if it is disjoint and if it remains disjoint sum after any base change \(T \to S\); it follows that for any couple \(i,j\), \(i \neq j\), objects \(S_i \times_S S_j\) are strict initial objects of \(A\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 4.5.1

In the category of sets, direct sums are disjoint and universal. It is therefore the same in any category of pre-bundles of sets (I 3.1) and consequently in any category of sheaves of sets on a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site(4.1); in particular, the initial object of \(\widetilde{C}\) is strict.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.6

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \((s_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a family of morphisms of \(C\). For any \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology \(\mathscr{C}\) on \(C\) (3.0.2), we denote by \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathscr{C}}\) the category of corresponding sheaves and by \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}} : C \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathscr{C}}\) the corresponding functor.

1) Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology such that \[ \coprod_{i \in I} \varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X_i) \xrightarrow{\ (\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(s_i))\ } \varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X) \] or an isomorphism. Then for any topology \(\mathscr{C}'\) finer than \(\mathscr{C}\), the morphism: \[ \coprod_{i \in I} \varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}'}(X_i) \xrightarrow{\ (\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}'}(s_i))\ } \varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}'}(X) \] is an isomorphism.

2) Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology on \(C\). The following properties (i) and (ii) are equivalents:

i) a) The family \((s_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), is covering for \(\mathscr{C}\).

b) For all \(i \in I\), the diagonal morphism of presheaves \(\Delta_i : X_i \hookrightarrow X_i \times_X X_i\) is transformed by the “associated bundle” functor (for \(\mathscr{C}\)) into a isomorphism (which is the case if the \(s_i\) are monomorphisms).

c) For any pair \((i,j)\), \(i \neq j\), of elements of \(I\), the prebundle \(X_i \times_X X_j\) is transformed by the "associated bundle" functor (for \(\mathscr{C}\)) into the initial object of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathscr{C}}\).

ii) \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X)\) is sum of \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X_i)\).
Proof.

1) A beam \(F\) for \(\mathscr{C}'\) is a beam for \(\mathscr{C}\). Consequently, for any beam \(F\) for \(\mathscr{C}'\), the morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X,F) \longrightarrow \prod_{i \in I} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(X_i,F) \] is an isomorphism, which results in the assertion.

2) By definition, we have the property ii) if and only if the morphism of presheaves \[ \Phi = (h(s_i), i \in I) : \coprod_i h(X_i) \longrightarrow h(X) \] is transformed by the “associated sheaf” functor into an isomorphism i.e. (4.2) if and only if \(a(\Phi)\) is an epimorphism and monomorphism of sheaves. According to (4.4), the morphism \(a(\Phi)\) is an epimorphism if and only if we have property a). Morphism diagonal \[ \Delta : \coprod_i h(X_i) \longrightarrow \Bigl(\coprod_i h(X_i)\Bigr) \times_{h(X)} \Bigl(\coprod_i h(X_i)\Bigr) \] is a direct sum of a family \(\Delta_{i,j}\), \((i,j) \in I \times I\), of morphisms defined as follows:
β) When \(i = j\), \(\Delta_{i,i}\) is the diagonal morphism \(h(X_i) \to h(X_i) \times_{h(X)} h(X_i)\).
γ) When \(i \neq j\), \(\Delta_{i,j}\) is the morphism \(\varnothing_{\widehat{C}} \to h(X_i) \times_{h(X)} h(X_j)\) (\(\varnothing_{\widehat{C}}\) designates the initial object of \(\widehat{C}\)).

The morphism \(a(\Phi)\) is a monomorphism if and only if \(a(\Delta)\) is a isomorphism, and according to (4.1) \(a(\Delta)\) is isomorphism if and only if the \(a(\Delta_{i,j})\) are isomorphisms i.e. if and only if we have properties b) and c).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \(X\) be an object of \(C\).

1) Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology on \(C\). The object \(X\) of \(C\) is transformed by the functor "associated beam" (for topology \(\mathscr{C}\)) to the initial object of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathscr{C}}\) if and only if the screen empty covers \(X\).

2) When \(X\) is a strict initial object of \(C\), the beam associated with \(X\), for any \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology finer than the canonical topology, is an initial object.
Proof.
1) We take for set \(I\) in 4.6 2) the empty set.

2) According to 1) and 4.6 1), it is enough to show that the empty sieve covers \(X\) for the canonical topology i.e. (2.6) it suffices to show that for any object \(Y \to X\) above \(X\), \(Y\) is an initial object of \(X\), which follows from definition (4.5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.2

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \((s_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a family of quarable morphisms of \(C\) with the same purpose having the following properties:

α) The family of \(s_i\), \(i \in I\), is covering.

β) For all \(i \in I\), \(s_i : X_i \to X\) is a monomorphism.

γ) For any pair \((i,j)\), \(i \neq j\), of elements of \(I\), \(X_i \times_X X_j\) is covered by the empty sieve, i.e. for any beam \(F\) on \(C\), \(F(X_i \times_X X_j)\) is a set reduced to one element.

Then \(\varepsilon_C(X)\) is sum of \(\varepsilon_C(X_i)\), \(i \in I\).
Proof. Follows immediately from 4.6 2) and 4.6.1 1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.3

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \((s_i : X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a family of quarable morphisms with the same goal. Suppose that the canonical topology of \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology. The following conditions are equivalent:

i) There exists a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology \(\mathscr{C}\) on \(C\), less fine-grained than the canonical topology, such that \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X)\) is the sum of \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X_i)\), \(i \in I\).

ii) The object \(X\) is a disjoint and universal sum (4.5) (in \(C\)) of \(X_i\).

Proof. ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). As \(X\) is the universal sum of \(X_i\), \(i \in I\), the family of \(s_i\), \(i \in I\), is covering for the canonical topology of \(C\) (2.6). The condition α) of 4.6.2 is therefore satisfied when we provide \(C\) with the canonical topology. The condition β) is obviously satisfied and the property γ) results from 4.6.1) 2), hence i).

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a topology on \(C\) less fine than the canonical topology such that \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X)\) is the sum of \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X_i)\). For any beam \(F\), for \(\mathscr{C}\), the canonical application \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \to \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,F)\) is a bijection. Furthermore, any representable pre-bundle is a beam. It immediately follows that \(X\) is sum in \(C\) of \(X_i\), \(i \in I\). Applying this to case where the set \(I\) is empty, we see that if an object of \(C\) is transformed into the initial object of \(\widetilde{C}\), this object is an initial object of \(C\). The functor \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\) is fully faithful and commutes at finite projective limits. Consequently, condition b) of 4.6 2) results in the \(s_i\), \(i \in I\), are monomorphisms of \(C\), and condition c) results, according to what precedes, that the \(X_i \times_X X_j\), \(i \neq j\), are initial objects of \(C\). Consequently \(X\) is the disjoint sum of \(X_i\). As \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}\) commutes to fiber products, this last property is stable under all change of base \(Y \to X\), and consequently \(X\) is a disjoint and universal sum of \(X_i\), \(i \in I\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \(X\) be an object of \(C\). Suppose that the canonical topology on \(C\) or a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology. The following properties are equivalents:

i) There exists on \(C\) a topology less fine than the canonical topology such that \(\varepsilon_{\mathscr{C}}(X)\) is an initial object of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathscr{C}}\).

ii) \(X\) is a strict initial object of \(C\).
Proof. We take for set \(I\) the empty set in 4.6.3.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.7

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(\widetilde{C}\) the category of the beams on \(C\) for the canonical topology, \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\) the canonical functor (4.4.0), \(R \rightrightarrows X\) a equivalence relation in \(C\) admitting a cokernel \(Y\) in \(C\). Let \(\pi : X \to Y\) be the morphism canonical and let's assume it is quarable. Consider then the following properties:

i) \(\varepsilon_C(Y)\) is the quotient of the equivalence relation \(\varepsilon_C(R) \rightrightarrows \varepsilon_C(X)\).

ii) The equivalence relation \(R \rightrightarrows X\) is effectively universal (cf. no. 7).

We have ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). When \(C\) has a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology less fine than the topology canonical, we have i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii).

Proof. ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). The canonical morphism \(R \to X \times_Y X\) is an isomorphism. Either \(S \hookrightarrow Y\) the sieve generated by \(\pi : X \to Y\). For any pre-beam \(F\), we have an exact diagram (I 2.12): \[ \mathrm{Hom}(S,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \rightrightarrows \mathrm{Hom}(R,F) . \] It is therefore sufficient to show that for any sheaf \(F\) for the canonical topology, the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(S,F) \] is a bijection i.e. it suffices to show that \(S\) is covering for the canonical topology, or although the sieve generated by \(\pi : X \to Y\) is covering, which results from definition 2.5.

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). The functor \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\) commutes at finite projective limits and is fully faithful. But the canonical morphism \(\varepsilon_C(R) \to \varepsilon_C(X) \times_{\varepsilon_C(Y)} \varepsilon_C(X)\) is an isomorphism (4.3). Consequently the morphism canonical \(R \to X \times_Y X\) is an isomorphism. The morphism \(\varepsilon_C(X) \to \varepsilon_C(Y)\) is a epimorphism, which leads (4.4) that \(\pi : X \to Y\) is a covering morphism of \(C\) for the canonical topology, cqfd.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.8

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. The beams category on \(C\) has the following properties:

a) Finite projective limits are representable.

b) The direct sums indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) are representable. They are disjointed and universal. (4.5)

c) Equivalence relations are effectively universal.
This was seen in 4.1, 2) and 3), 4.3, 3), and 4.5.1.

These properties have been highlighted because they will later allow us to characterize the \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories equivalent to categories of beams on categories belonging to \(\mathcal{U}\) (IV 1.2).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Noticed

Recall that property a) is equivalent to the property: there exists a final object, and the fiber products are representable (I 2.3.1).

Let \(C\) be a site whose underlying category is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. So the category \(\widetilde{C}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(C\) satisfies the conditions envisaged in I 7.3 ((i) or (ii), as desired), therefore by loc. cit. the various variants considered in I 7.1 for the notion of generating family in \(\widetilde{C}\) coincide. That said:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.10

With the previous notations, consider the functor canonical \(\varepsilon : C \to \widetilde{C}\) (4.4.0), and let \(G \subset \mathrm{ob}\, C\) be a topologically generating family in \(C\) (3.0.1). Then the family \((\varepsilon(X))_{X \in G}\) of objects of \(\widetilde{C}\) is a generating family. In in particular the family \((\varepsilon(X))_{X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C}\) of objects of \(C\) is generator.

It is necessary to prove that any morphism \(u : F \to F'\) in \(\widetilde{C}\) such that \[ (4.10.1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(\varepsilon(X),F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(\varepsilon(X),F') \] i.e. a bijection for all \(X \in G\), is an isomorphism. Or the previous application identifies with the application \[ (4.10.2) \qquad u(X) : F(X) \longrightarrow F'(X) , \] and it is necessary to show that if this is bijective for everything \(X \in G\), it is the same for everything \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\). Now let \((X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) be a covering family of \(X\) by objects of \(G\), and for all pair of indices \((i,j)\) of \(I\), consider the set of all morphisms \(X_{ijk} \to X_i \times_X X_j\) in \(\widetilde{C}\), with \(X_{ijk} \in G\) (\(k\) varying in a set of indices \(I_{i,j}\)). We then obtains a homomorphism of exact diagrams of sets \[ \xymatrix{ F(X) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \prod_i F(X_i) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar[d]^-{\wr} & \prod_{ijk} F(X_{ijk}) \ar[d]^-{\wr} \\ F'(X) \ar[r] & \prod_i F'(X_i) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \prod_{ijk} F'(X_{ijk}) } \] where by hypothesis the last two vertical arrows are bijections. It is therefore even from the first vertical arrow, cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.11

Suppose \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (3.0.2). So the category \(\widetilde{C}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and admits a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small generating family.

Indeed, by hypothesis we can take in 4.10 for \(G\) a small family generator, which proves the existence of a small generating family. On the other hand, if \(F, F' \in \mathrm{ob}\, \widetilde{C}\), a homomorphism of \(F\) in \(F'\) is known when we know the homomorphism (4.10.1) i.e. (4.10.2) for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, G\) (I 7.1.1), from which it follows that the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(F,F') \longrightarrow \prod_{X \in G} \mathrm{Hom}(F(X), F'(X)) \] is injective. As the second member is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, it is the same for the first, which proves that \(\widetilde{C}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.12

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. Then for any object of \(\widetilde{C}\), the set subobjects of \(X\) and the set of quotient objects of \(X\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.
This results from I 7.4 resp. I 7.5, which apply through 4.11.

footnotes.html: (∗) When the site \(C\) does not necessarily have a small topologically generating family and when the topology of \(C\) is less fine than the canonical topology, we have ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i) (see demonstration of 4.4).

pages.title: 5. Extension of a topology from \(C\) to \(\widehat{C}\)

blocks.html: 5. Extending a topology from \(C\) to \(\widehat{C}\)

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(f : H \to K\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

i) For all \(X \to K\), with \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the corresponding morphism \(H \times_K X \to X\) has the image of a sieve covering \(X\).

ii) The morphism \(a(f) : aH \to aK\) on the associated sheaves is an epimorphism by \(\widetilde{C}\).

ii bis) For any beam \(F\) on \(C\), the application \(\mathrm{Hom}(K,F) \to \mathrm{Hom}(H,F)\) deduced from \(f\) is injective.
Proof. It is clear that (ii) is equivalent to (ii bis).

Let us prove that (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). Under 4.4, (i) means that \(u : a(H \times_K X) \to a(X)\) is an epimorphism. However, as \(a(H \times_K X) \simeq a(H) \times_{a(K)} a(X)\), \(a\) commuting to finite \(\varprojlim\) (4.1), the morphism considered is deduced from \(a(f) : a(H) \to a(K)\) by change of base. Like the epimorphisms of \(\widetilde{C}\) are universal, this shows that (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). Conversely, as the family of \(X_i \to K\) is epimorphic, it is the same for the family of \(a(X) \to a(K)\) in \(\widetilde{C}\) (4.1), therefore for check that \(a(f) : a(H) \to a(K)\) is epimorphic, just see it after any change base of the previous type \(a(X) \to a(K)\), which proves i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii), cqfd.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.2

1) A morphism \(f : H \to K\) satisfying the three conditions equivalents of 5.1 is called a covering morphism. A family of morphisms \(f_i : H_i \to K\), \(i \in I\), with the same goal is said to be covering if the corresponding morphism \(f : \coprod_i H_i \to K\) is covering.

2) A morphism \(f : H \to K\) is said to be bi-covering if it is covering and if the morphism diagonal \(H \to H \times_K H\) is covering. A family \(f_i : H_i \to K\), \(i \in I\), with the same purpose is said to be bicovering if the corresponding morphism \(f : \coprod_i H_i \to K\) is bicovering.

By condition i) of 5.1, say that a family \((f_i : H_i \to K)\), \(i \in I\), is covering, means that for any morphism \(X \to K\), with \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the family of \(H_i \times_K X \to X\), \(i \in I\), has as image a sieve covering \(X\); or again, by ii), the family of morphisms \(a(f_i) : aH_i \to aK\) in \(\widetilde{C}\) is epimorphic (given that the functor \(a\) commutes to direct sums (4.1))(∗).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.3

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(f : H \to K\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

i) The morphism \(f\) is bicovering.

i bis) The morphism \(f\) is covering and for any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any pair of morphisms \(u,v : X \rightrightarrows H\) such that \(fu = fv\), the kernel of \((u,v)\) is a spanning sieve of \(X\).

ii) The morphism \(a(f) : aH \to aK\) is an isomorphism of \(\widetilde{C}\).

ii bis) For any beam \(F\) on \(C\), the application \(\mathrm{Hom}(K,F) \to \mathrm{Hom}(H,F)\) is a bijection.

Proof. The equivalence i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) i bis) results from condition i) of 5.1, applied to diagonal morphism \(H \to H \times_K H\); the equivalence ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) ii bis) is trivial.

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). The morphism \(a(f) : aH \to aK\) is an epimorphism (5.1). Like the functor \(a\) commutes to the formation of fiber products (4.1), the diagonal morphism \(aH \to aH \times_{aK} aH\) is an epimorphism (5.1). As the diagonal morphism is always a monomorphism is an isomorphism (4.2). Consequently the morphism \(a(f)\) is a monomorphism. It is therefore an isomorphism (4.2).

ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). As the functor \(a\) commutes to the formation of fiber products, the morphism \(f\) and the diagonal morphism \(H \to H \times_K H\) are transformed by \(a\) into isomorphisms. In particular, they are transformed by \(a\) into epimorphisms. They are therefore coverings, cqfd.

It follows from 5.3, and from the fact that \(a\) commutes to direct sums, that a family \((f_i : H_i \to K)\), \(i \in I\), of morphisms of \(\widehat{C}\) is bicovering if and only if the \(f_i\) induce on the associated sheaves an isomorphism of the direct sum \(\coprod_i aH_i\) on \(aK\), or even if and only if, for any beam \(F\), the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(K,F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(H_i,F) \] is bijective.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. There exists on \(\widehat{C}\) a topology (obviously unique) such that a family \(H_i \to K\) of arrows of \(\widehat{C}\) of the same purpose is covering for this topology if and only if it is covering in the sense of 5.2. It is also the topology the least fine on \(\widehat{C}\) among the topologies \(T\) having the following properties:

a) \(T\) is finer than the canonical topology of \(\widehat{C}\) (i.e. any family epimorphic of \(\widehat{C}\) is covering for \(T\)).

b) Any covering family in \(C\) is covering in \(\widehat{C}\).

Proof. We will limit ourselves to giving indications. We leave it to the reader to show that the covering families within the meaning of 5.2 are the covering families of a topology \(T_C\) on \(\widehat{C}\) (we use 4.1). The covering families of the canonical topology on \(\widehat{C}\) are the epimorphic families on \(\widehat{C}\) (2.6 and I 3.1). As \(a\) commutes at the limits inductive (4.1), the topology \(T_C\) is finer than the canonical topology of \(\widehat{C}\). In addition the covering families of \(C\) are covering families of \(T_C\) (5.1). If therefore \(T'\) designates the less fine of the topologies of \(\widehat{C}\) having the properties a) and b), \(T_C\) is finer than \(T'\). Let \((f_i : H_i \to K)\), \(i \in I\), be a covering family of \(T_C\). Let us show that \((f_i, i \in I)\) is a family covering of \(T'\). Let \(s_i : H_i \to H = \coprod H_i\) be the canonical monomorphisms and \(f = (f_i, i \in I) : H = \coprod_i H_i \to K\) the morphism defined by \(f_i\). The family of \(s_i\) is covering for \(T'\). To show that \((f_i, i \in I)\) is a covering family of \(T'\), it is therefore sufficient to show, by virtue of the axiom (T2) of topologies, that the morphism \(f : H \to K\) is covering for \(T'\). There is an epimorphic family of \(\widehat{C}\), \(u_\lambda : X_\lambda \to K\), \(\lambda \in \Lambda\), \(X_\lambda \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\) (I 3.4). To show that \(f : H \to K\) is covering for \(T'\), it is therefore sufficient, by virtue of the axiom (T 2) of topologies, to show that for all \(\lambda \in \Lambda\), the morphism \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : H \times_K X_\lambda \to X_\lambda\) is covering for \(T'\). Let then be \(v_j : Y_j \to H \times_K X_\lambda\), \(j \in J\), \(Y_j \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), an epimorphic family of \(\widehat{C}\). The \((\mathrm{pr}_2 \circ v_j, j \in J)\) family is covering for \(T_C\). It is therefore a covering family of \(C\) (5.1). It is therefore a covering family of \(T'\). By Subsequently the sieve generated by \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : H \times_K X_\lambda \to X_\lambda\) contains a covering sieve for \(T'\). He is therefore covering for \(T'\) and consequently \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : H \times_K X_\lambda \to X_\lambda\) is covering, cqfd.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.4.1

The proof of 5.4 actually shows that any topology \(T'\) on \(\widehat{C}\), finer than the canonical topology of \(\widehat{C}\), is the least fine of the topologies \(T\) on \(\widehat{C}\) which have the following properties:

a) \(T\) is finer than the canonical topology of \(\widehat{C}\).

b) Any covering family for \(T'\) of the form \(u_i : X_i \to X\), \(i \in I\), where \(X\) and the \(X_i\) are objects of \(C\), is covering for \(T\).

In particular, any topology \(T'\) on \(\widehat{C}\), finer than the canonical topology, is only determined by the families \(u_i : X_i \to X\), \(i \in I\), \(X_i\) and \(X\) objects of \(C\), which are covering for \(T'\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.4.2

We can easily show that for any topology \(T'\) on \(\widehat{C}\), finer than the canonical topology, the set of families of morphisms \((X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), of the same purpose of \(C\), which are covering for \(T'\), is the set of covering families of a topology on \(C\). So 5.4 and 5.4.1 make it possible to establish a one-to-one correspondence between topologies on \(C\) and topologies on \(\widehat{C}\) finer than the canonical topology.

Let \(C\) be a small category. Let us designate by \(\mathrm{Caf}\) all the subcategories strictly full (any object isomorphic to an object of the subcategory is an object of the subcategory) of \(\widehat{C}\) whose injection functor admits a left adjoint which commutes at finite projective limits. Let us also designate by \(\mathscr{T}\) the set of topologies on \(C\). The Theorem 3.4 defines an application for us: \[ \Phi : \mathscr{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Caf} . \] We will define an application in reverse. For this, it is necessary to associate with everything element \(e = (i' : C' \rightleftarrows \widehat{C}\), \(a'\) added to the left of \(i')\) a topology \(T_e\) on \(C\). For everything object \(X\) of \(C\), we will define \(J_e(X)\) as being the set of sub-objects of \(X\) whose injection morphism is transformed by \(a'\) into an isomorphism. We immediately check, using the hypotheses made on \(a'\), we thus define a topology \(T_e\) on \(C\). We therefore have defined an application: \[ \Psi : \mathrm{Caf} \longrightarrow \mathscr{T} . \] We then have the result (due to J. GIRAUD):

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.5

The map \(\Phi\) is a bijection, and \(\Psi\) is the map reverse.

Proof. Application \(\Psi \circ \Phi\) is the identity. In fact, this immediately results from 5.1. Application \(\Phi \circ \Psi\) is the identity. Indeed, let \((i' : C' \rightleftarrows \widehat{C})\) be an element of \(\mathrm{Caf}\), \(T_e\) the topology that corresponds to it by \(\Psi\), \(C_e\) the category of beams for \(T_e\). We then demonstrates, using the definition of the topology \(T_e\) and the definition of morphisms bi-coverings (5.2), the equivalence of the following assertions:
i) The morphism \(u\) of \(\widehat{C}\) is bicovering for the topology \(T_e\).
ii) The morphism \(u\) of \(\widehat{C}\) is transformed by \(a'\) into an isomorphism.

It is clear that \(C'\) is a full subcategory of \(C_e\). It is therefore sufficient to show that any beam \(F\) for topology \(T_e\) is an object of \(C'\). But, according to the equivalence above, the morphism \(F \to i' \circ a'(F)\) is bicovering, and its source and its goal being bundles, we deduce by 5.3 (ii) that \(F \to i' \circ a'(F)\) is an isomorphism, cqfd.

footnotes.html: (∗) Note that the property for a morphism or a family of morphisms of \(\widehat{C}\) of being covering (resp. bicovering) is stable by change of base.

blocks.html: 6. Value bundles in a category
Let \(C\) and \(D\) be two categories. A contravariant functor of \(C\) in \(D\), \(F : C^\circ \to D\), is called a pre-bundle on \(C\) with values in \(D\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

Let \(C\) be a site, \(D\) be a category. A pre-bundle \(F : C^\circ \to D\) is called a bundle on \(C\) with values in \(D\) (or, more briefly, a bundled with values in \(D\)) if for any object \(S\) of \(D\), the pre-bundle of sets: \[ X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_D(S, F(X)) , \qquad X \in \mathrm{ob}(C) \] is a beam. The full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ, D)\) formed from the bundles on \(C\) to values in \(D\) will be noted \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}^{\sim}(C^\circ, D)\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 6.2

1) Condition 6.1 means that for any object \(X\) of \(C\), any sieve covering \(R\) of \(X\), and any object \(S\) of \(D\), we have an isomorphism (I 3.5 and 2.1): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_D(S, F(X)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C/R} F(\,\cdot\,) . \] We thus find, when \(D\) is the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets, the definition of the sheaves of sets (loc. cit.).

2) Let \(D'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \(G : D \to D'\) be a limit-commuting functor projectives. The functor \(G\) transforms, by composition, the sheaves with values in \(D\) into bundles with value in \(D'\).

Let \(\gamma\) be a kind of algebraic structure defined by finite projective limits (I 2.9) and \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\) the category of \(\gamma\)-sets which belong to \(\mathcal{U}\). Let us designate by \(\mathrm{esj} : \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) the “underlying set” functor. The functor \(\mathrm{esj}\) commutes to projective limits and, consequently, according to the previous remark, defined by composition of functors: \[ \mathrm{esj}^\wedge : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma) \longrightarrow \widehat{C} \] \[ \mathrm{esj}^\sim : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}^{\sim}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma) \longrightarrow \widetilde{C} . \] The \(\mathrm{esj}^\sim\) functor is called the “underlying set bundle” functor. In fact, he factors canonically by the category \(\gamma\text{-}\widetilde{C}\) of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\widetilde{C}\), and again designating by \(\widetilde{\mathrm{esj}}\) the functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}^{\sim}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma) \longrightarrow \gamma\text{-}\widetilde{C} \] obtained, we can state:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.1

The functor \(\mathrm{esj}^\sim\) establishes an equivalence of categories between the category of beams on \(C\) with value in \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\), that of \(\gamma\)-objects of the category of bundles of sets, and the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of the category of pre-beams of assemblies whose underlying pre-beam is a beam.
Proof. The proof essentially uses (I 3.2) and the fact that a projective limit finite number of bundles in the category of presheaves is a bundle (4.1).

The previous proposition justifies the abuse of language which consists of identifying a value beam in \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\) and the corresponding \(\gamma\)-object in \(\widetilde{C}\). We will now do systematically this abuse of language. We will use the classic terminology: group bundles, commutative group bundles (which we will most likely call often abelian bundles), ring bundles, bundles of \(A\)-modules...

We designate by \[ \widetilde{C}_\gamma \quad (\text{resp. } \widehat{C}_\gamma) \] the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\widetilde{C}\) (resp. of \(\widehat{C}\)). When \(\gamma\) is the structure species “\(A\)-module”, we also write \(\widetilde{C}_A\), or simply \(\widetilde{C}_{ab}\) when \(A = \mathbb{Z}\).
Suppose \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. The “associated beam” functor is left exact (4.1) and subsequently:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.4

The inclusion functor \(i_\gamma : \widetilde{C}_\gamma \to \widehat{C}_\gamma\) admits a left adjoint \(a_\gamma\) exact on the left, i.e. we have an isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}_\gamma}(a_\gamma\,,\ ) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}_\gamma}(\ ,\ i_\gamma) . \] Let \(X\) be an object of \(\widehat{C}_\gamma\). The bundle of sets underlying \(a_\gamma(X)\) is canonically isomorphic to the bundle associated with the presheaf of sets underlying \(X\). The morphism of pre-sheaves of sets underlying the addition morphism \(\mathrm{id} \to i_\gamma a_\gamma\) identifies with the addition morphism applied to the set prebundle underlying.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.5

Suppose the functor \(\mathrm{esj} : \gamma\text{-}\mathcal{U} \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) admits an assistant on the left(∗): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}}(.\,, \mathrm{esj}(.)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\gamma\text{-}\mathcal{U}}(\mathrm{Lib}(.),.) . \] The functor \(\mathrm{esj}^\sim : \widetilde{C}_\gamma \to \widetilde{C}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{esj}^\wedge : \widehat{C}_\gamma \to \widehat{C}\)) admits a left adjoint \(\mathrm{Lib}^\sim\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Lib}^\wedge\)) and we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Lib}^\sim = a_\gamma \mathrm{Lib}^\wedge i . \]
Proof. The proof is formal and is left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6

Let \((X_i)\), \(i \in I\), a generating family of \(\widetilde{C}\) (4.9). So the \(\mathrm{Lib}^\sim(X_i)\) family is a generating family of \(\widetilde{C}_\gamma\).

Proof. The proof is formal once we notice that the functor \(\mathrm{esj}^\sim\) commutes to projective limits and that it is conservative (\(\mathrm{esj}^\sim(u)\) is a isomorphism \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(u\) is isomorphism).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.7

Let \(A\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle of rings, or else a small ring, \(\widetilde{C}_A\) (resp. \(\widehat{C}_A\)) the category of beams (resp. pre-beams) of \(A\)-unit modules (6.3.3) on a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\). Then \(\widetilde{C}_A\) is an \(\mathcal{U}\)-abelian category satisfying the axioms (AB 5) (“existence of exact filtering inductive limits on the left”) and (AB 3)* (“existence of infinite products”) from [TOHOKU]. It has a family of generators indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Proof. It is clear that the category \(\widehat{C}_A\) is an abelian category verifying the axioms (AB 3), (AB 4), and (AB 5) (I 2.8 and I 3.3). We then deduce from 6.4 that the category \(\widetilde{C}_A\) is an additive category where cores and co-cores are representable. More precisely, be \(i_A\) and \(a_A\) the inclusion functors in the presheaves and associated sheaves and \(u : X \to Y\) a morphism of \(\widetilde{C}_A\). The functor \(a_A\) is left exact and commutes at the limits inductive. The functor \(i_A\) commutes at projective limits. Consequently the functor \(i_A a_A : \widehat{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_A\) is a left-exact additive functor. We deduce isomorphisms canonicals: \[ (*) \qquad i_A(\mathrm{Ker}(u)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ker}(i_A(u)) , \] \[ (**) \qquad \mathrm{coker}(u) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } a_A \mathrm{coker}(i_A(u)) . \] We then have a canonical isomorphism: \[ (***) \qquad \mathrm{coim}(u) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } a_A \mathrm{coim}(i_A(u)) . \] Let us show that the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{coim}(u) \to \mathrm{im}(u)\) is an isomorphism. For this, he suffice to show, according to (*), that the sequence: \[ 0 \to i_A(\mathrm{coim}(u)) \to i_A(Y) \to i_A(\mathrm{coker}(u)) \] is correct. Using the isomorphisms (**) and (***) and noticing that \(a_A i_A(Y)\) is isomorphic at \(Y\), we see that this sequence is isomorphic to the sequence: \[ 0 \to i_A a_A(\mathrm{coim}(i_A(u))) \to i_A a_A i_A(Y) \to i_A a_A(\mathrm{coker}(i_A(u))) , \] which is none other than the transform by the functor \(i_A a_A\) of the sequence: \[ 0 \to \mathrm{coim}(i_A(u)) \to i_A(Y) \to \mathrm{coker}(i_A(u)) . \] However, this sequence is exact and the functor \(i_A a_A\) is exact on the left. The category \(\widetilde{C}_A\) is therefore an abelian category. The functor \(a_A : \widehat{C}_A \to \widetilde{C}_A\) is left exact and commutes to any inductive limits. Consequently it is additive, exact and commutes at the limits inductive. The category \(\widehat{C}_A\) verifying the axiom (AB 5), it is the same for the category \(\widetilde{C}_A\). Finally it is clear that in the category \(\widetilde{C}_A\), the products indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) are representable and, consequently, that the axiom (AB 3)* is verified, which completes the demonstration of the first assertion.

For any ring \(A\) element of \(\mathcal{U}\), the functor: \[ \mathrm{esj} : \mathcal{U}\text{-}A\text{-module} \longrightarrow \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens} \] admits a left adjoint \(X_A\) (\(A\)-generated free module). So just apply 4.10 and 6.6 to complete the proof.

blocks.title: Rating
blocks.html:
Rating 6.8

Let \(H\) be a sheaf of sets. The \(A\)-module bundle associated with the pre-beam (see notation 6.5) \[ X \mapsto \mathrm{Lib}_A H(X) \qquad X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C \] is noted \(A_H\). When \(H = a(X)\) (beam associated with the pre-beam represented by \(X\)) we write sometimes simply \(A_X\) (by abuse of notation). The proof of 6.7 shows that the bundle family of \(A\)-module \(A_X\), (\(X \in \mathrm{ob}(C)\)), is a generating family, indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), of the category of bundles of \(A\)-modules.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 6.9

According to [TOHOKU], 6.7 shows that the category \(\widetilde{C}_A\), when \(A\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), has sufficient injectives. We know, moreover, that the products infinites are not necessarily exact in \(\widetilde{C}_A\), and, consequently, that the category \(\widetilde{C}_A\) does not, in general, admit sufficient projectives [ROOS].

footnotes.html: (∗) We demonstrate that such an adjoint always exists [C.F.]: free group, free abelian group, \(A\)-free module... .
